quote: I was told that solar will not work on my house
stewart_read: This is the root-cause moment of the call. Carrie surfaces a prior Ion DQ in her third utterance. The schema home_disqualified rebuttal calls for 'Do you know why it was disqualified?' — a curiosity-opening move that Bucky did not execute. Instead he challenged the DQ via satellite map. The deeper issue is a CRM/lead-routing gap: Ion's own prior consultation result was not visible to Bucky before he dialed, which means he was set up to re-litigate a decision his own company had already made.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky challenged the DQ because he genuinely saw something on the map that suggested the prior assessment was wrong. Rejected — the manager hold at 03:57 confirmed the original DQ, and Bucky's map read produced no new information.
coaching_implication: Two coaching threads here. First, drill the home_disqualified rebuttal — Bucky should have opened with 'Do you know why?' before challenging anything. Second, escalate to operations: if Ion's CRM holds prior consultation outcomes, setters should see that data before dialing recycled leads. This is a lead-routing process gap, not just a rep skill gap.

quote: you guys keep calling me and texting me.
stewart_read: Carrie's contact-frequency complaint is a trust signal — she is telling Bucky that Ion has already eroded goodwill through repeated outreach. The schema calls for honest specificity to reduce suspicion. Bucky's response ('I just I'm a little...') is a verbal stumble that neither acknowledges the complaint nor resolves it. He moves immediately to the map challenge, leaving the friction unaddressed.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky simply didn't have a prepared response to the contact-frequency complaint. Supported — there is no recovery attempt, no acknowledgment, no pivot to action.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the analysis_directives.friction_resolution_speed standard: non-business complaints should be resolved via action, not defense or dismissal. A one-line acknowledgment ('I hear you — let me make sure we get this sorted') before moving to the map would have reduced Carrie's defensive posture. The complaint was never resolved on this call.

quote: Oh, no. Our house is north south facing. You can't put anything on the front of the house because of the HOA.
stewart_read: Carrie is delivering a multi-factor technical home analysis: orientation, HOA restriction, shade coverage, surface area. This is informed-objection territory, not a vulnerability disclosure. Bucky has no structured response — he is being out-argued on technical content by the customer. The setter's scope discipline calls for bridging technical questions to the specialist rather than engaging them directly. Bucky had no bridge available because he had already committed to the map-challenge path.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky was trying to find any viable path before conceding the DQ. Partially plausible, but the schema setter_scope_discipline standard is clear — technical home analysis is closer-tier content. The correct move was to acknowledge the detail and route to the specialist, not to improvise a counter-analysis.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to reinforce scope discipline. When a customer delivers a technical home analysis this detailed, the setter's job is not to out-argue them — it is to say 'That's exactly the kind of detail our specialist needs to evaluate. Let me get you connected.' On a prior-DQ lead, that bridge also opens the door to the roofing cross-sell.

quote: I've been told by another power company. I mean, another solar company the same thing.
stewart_read: This is the second touch of the prior-DQ pattern — now confirmed by a second independent solar company. The schema home_disqualified rebuttal does not have a canonical response for multi-source DQ confirmation; this is a schema gap. Bucky's response is to immediately escalate to the manager hold rather than acknowledge the second-source confirmation or attempt any pivot. The multi-source confirmation materially changes the recovery calculus — at this point the solar DQ is structurally closed, and the only remaining value move is the roofing cross-sell.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky escalated to the manager because he recognized he was out of his depth and needed authority to either confirm or override the DQ. Plausible — and partially supported by the civil exit tone at 04:01, suggesting the hold did not damage the relationship. However, the hold returned no new value and the roofing bridge was never attempted.
coaching_implication: Flag this as a schema gap for Kenny: Ion needs a canonical response for multi-source DQ confirmation. The current home_disqualified rebuttal ('Do you know why?') is designed for single-source DQs. When two companies have said the same thing, the curiosity-opening move is exhausted — the pivot should be to the roofing cross-sell bridge, not a manager hold.

quote: And my husband, who's an engineer, is like, there's there's just no way. You can call as many power solar companies as you want.
stewart_read: Carrie's husband is introduced here as a technical authority who has already evaluated and rejected solar for this property. This is not a spouse-coordination protocol moment (no appointment was being booked), but it is a verify.title_co_owners signal — the husband is a co-decision-maker whose position is already fixed. Bucky does not acknowledge the husband's role or expertise at any point. On a call where recovery was still theoretically possible (roofing cross-sell), the husband's engineering background is actually a potential credibility bridge — an engineer who has evaluated the home's solar limitations might be receptive to a roofing conversation framed around structural planning.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky simply didn't register the husband as a decision-maker because the call was already in DQ territory. Supported — there is no acknowledgment of the husband at any point.
coaching_implication: Use this to reinforce the verify.title_co_owners discipline even on calls that appear to be heading toward DQ. Knowing the husband is an engineer and a co-decision-maker is information that changes the roofing cross-sell pitch — 'an engineer would appreciate that we do roofing and solar as an integrated plan' is a different entry point than a generic bridge.

quote: So let me ask my manager if you're willing to because you're willing to trim up if it'll work. Just I'll go find out right now.
stewart_read: The manager hold is the last moment before the call closes where a cross-sell bridge was structurally available. Bucky uses the hold to confirm the solar DQ — which was the right operational outcome — but returns with only the DQ confirmation and no roofing bridge. The schema cross_sell_signals section is explicit: solar disqualified by physical constraints = potential roof sale + future solar sale. The hold gave Bucky 54 seconds and a manager conversation — neither produced the bridge. The civil exit at 04:01 ('Thank you,' 'You have a great day') suggests Carrie was not hostile at close, which means the roofing bridge would have landed in a receptive moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky's manager hold was a deliberate move to get authoritative confirmation before conceding, preserving credibility with Carrie. Plausible — and the civil exit partially supports it. However, the absence of any cross-sell attempt after the hold means the hold produced no incremental value beyond what Carrie had already told Bucky at 00:17.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-revenue coaching moment on the call. Walk Bucky through the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned bridge. The suggested rephrase after the manager hold: 'You were right — solar isn't the right fit for your roof setup. One thing I want to mention before I let you go: Ion does roofing in-house, and a lot of customers in your situation find it useful to have someone take a look at the roof separately. Would it be worth a quick conversation with our roofing team?' The civil exit tone confirms Carrie would have heard this.

quote: you were right. We're not allowed to do anything because it is too shaky.
stewart_read: Bucky's DQ confirmation is the correct outcome but the delivery is weak — 'too shaky' is imprecise language that may read as uncertain rather than authoritative. More importantly, the confirmation is delivered with no cross-sell bridge, no graceful exit language, and no removal-from-list acknowledgment. Carrie had explicitly requested list removal at 04:01 ('Please please remove me from the list') — Bucky's 'I gotcha' is the only acknowledgment, and it is not a committed action.
coaching_implication: Two things to drill: (1) DQ confirmation language should be clean and authoritative, not hedged ('too shaky' is not a professional close). (2) List-removal requests are a protocol moment — Bucky should have confirmed the action explicitly ('I'm noting that right now — you won't hear from us again') rather than a casual 'I gotcha.' The latter leaves Carrie uncertain whether the request was actually logged.

quote: Okay. Alright. Thank you. Please please remove me from the list. I gotcha. You have a great day. Thanks. Alright. Bye bye.
stewart_read: Carrie's exit is civil — 'Thank you,' 'You have a great day' — despite arriving with a contact-frequency complaint and a prior DQ. This is a meaningful signal: Bucky did not escalate the relationship damage, and the call ended without hostility. This partially supports the read that the manager hold, while reactive, served a de-escalation function by demonstrating that Bucky was willing to check rather than argue. The civility of the exit is the one positive trajectory signal on this call.
alternative_hypothesis: The civil exit could reflect Carrie's baseline politeness rather than any rep-driven rapport repair. Plausible — there is no specific moment where Bucky did something that visibly shifted Carrie's tone toward warmth. The exit civility is consistent with her opening tone (cold but not hostile) rather than evidence of repair.
coaching_implication: Surface this for Bucky as a positive signal — he did not make things worse, and Carrie's civility at close means the roofing bridge would have had a receptive landing. Use it to frame the cross-sell coaching: 'You ended on good terms — that was the moment to offer the roofing conversation. She would have heard it.'
